title: Compound Events
subtitle: Two-step experiments and independent events
intro: Compound events combine more than one action, like flipping two coins or rolling two dice. To find their probabilities, first list the full sample space: with two coins there are 2 × 2 = 4 outcomes, and with two dice there are 6 × 6 = 36 ordered outcomes.

These actions are independent — the first result doesn't change the second — so you can multiply individual probabilities. You'll count favorable outcomes for events like "exactly one head," "at least one head," or a particular dice sum, and see why some sums (like 7) are far more likely than others.

questions.question: Rolling two dice, P(a 6 on the first AND a 6 on the second) = 1/6 × 1/6 = 1/36. What is P(a 6 on the first OR a 6 on the second), i.e. at least one 6?

remediation.question: Of the 36 outcomes, how many show at least one 6 (a 6 on either die)?
Flipping two coins, P(heads AND heads) = 1/4. What is P(heads on the first OR heads on the second), i.e. at least one head?

questions.question: What is the probability that the sum of two dice is greater than 9 (a sum of 10, 11, or 12)?

remediation.question: The outcomes with a sum above 9 are 10 → (4,6),(5,5),(6,4); 11 → (5,6),(6,5); 12 → (6,6). That is 6 outcomes out of 36, which simplifies to:
What is the probability that the sum of two dice is exactly 12 (only the outcome 6 and 6)?
